coredata.dc:title: Application of sono-photochemical methods to cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots loaded with indium phthalocyanine for enhanced singlet oxygen generation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gökçil, Gökçe
Celep, Kevser
Şen, Pınar
Şahin, Fikrettin
Erdoğmuş, Ali
Atmaca, Göknur Yaşa
coredata.dc:description: Existing photodynamic therapy (PDT) agents face challenges such as low singlet oxygen production, severe photobleaching, and poor biocompatibility. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel method combining newly synthesized phthalocyanine with cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (cys-GQDs). This approach aims to enhance singlet oxygen production, solubility and stability in biological environments. In this study a new indium phthalocyanine and its cys-GQDs (InPc@cys-GQDs) derivative were synthesized as a new sono-photosensitizer candidate for therapeutic applications. Their photochemical and sono-photochemical properties were evaluated, focusing on singlet oxygen generation efficiency. Additionally, the stability, photostability, cellular uptake, and biocompatibility of these agents were tested through in vitro assays. The results show that the InPc@cys-GQDs conjugate has significantly higher singlet oxygen yield, especially with sono-photodynamic method compared to only phthalocyanine agent. They also demonstrate improved cellular internalization and reduced cytotoxicity, suggesting they offer a promising advancement for clinical PDT applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phthalocyanines
Photodynamic Therapy (PDT)
Singlet oxygen
Sono-photodynamic therapy
Graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized electronic absorption spectra of cys-GQDs, InPc and InPc@cys-GQDs.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) showing average particle sizes of (a) cys-GQDs, (b) InPc@cys-GQDs.
The comparative FT-IR spectra of cys-GQDs, InPc and InPc@cys-GQDs.
X-ray diffraction patterns of (a) cys-GQDs, (b) InPc, (c) InPc@cys-GQDs.
Absorption (694 nm), emission (709 nm) and excitation (701 nm) spectra of InPc in DMSO.
The change in UV–Vis spectrum of the singlet oxygen quantum yield measurement of InPc a) by photochemical study b) by sono-photochemical study.
The change in UV–Vis spectrum of the singlet oxygen quantum yield measurement of InPc@cys-GQDs a) by photochemical study b) by sono-photochemical study.
The change in UV–Vis spectrum of photodegradation quantum efficiency measurement of InPc.
The change in UV–Vis spectrum of photodegradation quantum efficiency measurement of InPc@cys-GQDs.
Synthetic route of new indium phthalocyanine; Reagents and conditions; (i) DMF, K2CO3, 24 h; (ii) InCl3, penthanol, DBU, argon atm, 120 °C, 24 h.
An illustration of π-π stacking of InPc onto graphene quantum dots: (i) sonicate, 4 h, stir room temperature, 4 d.
Spectral parameters concerning the photophysical and photochemical properties of the complexes.
Physicochemical and Photochemical Properties of Different Complexes in DMSO.

head.ce:title: Application of sono-photochemical methods to cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots loaded with indium phthalocyanine for enhanced singlet oxygen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yildiz Technical University, Esenler, Istanbul 34210, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yildiz Technical University, Esenler, Istanbul 34210, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Science and Technology, Turkish-German University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Science and Technology, Turkish-German University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetics and Bioengineering, Yeditepe University, Ataşehir, Istanbul 34755, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Üsküdar University, 34662 Üsküdar,Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Üsküdar University, 34662 Üsküdar,Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Health Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence, 34220 Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Health Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Health Biotechnology Joint Research and Application Center of Excellence, 34220 Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Therapeutic studies were aimed to be carried out on nanoconjugated novel zinc and indium phthalocyanines. For this purpose, photophysical-chemical and sono-photochemical properties of zinc and indium phthalocyanines alone and Pc-conjugated to GQD nanomaterial were investigated.

Existing photodynamic therapy (PDT) agents face challenges such as low singlet oxygen production, severe photobleaching, and poor biocompatibility. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel method combining newly synthesized phthalocyanine with cysteine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (cys-GQDs). This approach aims to enhance singlet oxygen production, solubility and stability in biological environments.

In this study a new indium phthalocyanine and its cys-GQDs (InPc@cys-GQDs) derivative were synthesized as a new sono-photosensitizer candidate for therapeutic applications. Their photochemical and sono-photochemical properties were evaluated, focusing on singlet oxygen generation efficiency. Additionally, the stability, photostability, cellular uptake, and biocompatibility of these agents were tested through in vitro assays. The results show that the InPc@cys-GQDs conjugate has significantly higher singlet oxygen yield, especially with sono-photodynamic method compared to only phthalocyanine agent. They also demonstrate improved cellular internalization and reduced cytotoxicity, suggesting they offer a promising advancement for clinical PDT applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been used since the 1990s as an alternative treatment method for cancer, which has a high mortality rate [1–3]. The working principle of PDT is based on the ensuring the transition between systems and formation of singlet oxygen because of irradiating the photosensitizer with the help of visible light [3–6]. This singlet oxygen is transferred to the cancer cell, causing apoptosis or necrosis, and killing the cancer cells [7–10]. This method has attracted a lot of attention since it does not have major side effects or a treatment process that complicates the patient's life, unlike other traditional treatment methods. In addition, it has many advantages such as being able to be applied without the need for surgical intervention, natural site selectivity due to accumulation in tumor cells, low toxicity, low cost, reproducibility, and rapidity [11,12]. It also has the disadvantage that light cannot penetrate deeply into living tissues. This disadvantage has limited the method to superficial lesions [12,13]. To overcome this disadvantage and improve the method, sonodynamic treatment (SDT) with ultrasound-activated sensitizers has been created since ultrasound can penetrate deeper. The SDT method is remarkably like PDT. The main difference between them is that SDT is activated by ultrasound instead of light [13–16]. Another difference that distinguishes the two methods is that the concentration of sonosensitizer that should be used in SDT is much higher than the amount of photosensitizer used in PDT. This situation prevented the method from being approved for clinical applications, despite the advantage of deep penetration of SDT [5,7,17]. A combined method called sono-photodynamic therapy (SPDT) has been developed to eliminate the disadvantages of these two methods and benefit from their advantages together [18,19]. According to the literature, this combined treatment provides higher anticancer results than either monotherapy [20,21]. The SPDT method uses a sensitizer that can be activated by ultrasound and light to generate photochemical and sonochemical reactions [22,23]. Effective SPDT studies depend on the sensitizer selected, the brightness of the light, and the frequency of the ultrasound. The sensitizers should have a good clearance from healthy tissues, stable chemical composition, long-wavelength absorption, high sono-photo sensitivity and not be poisonous [23,24]. Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are frequently used sensitizers in SPDT to obtain high anticancer results due to their superior photophysical and photochemical properties such as deeper tissue penetration, high triplet quantum yield, excellent absorbance at long wavelengths, chemical stability and lack of dark toxicity [25–27]. To make Pcs better suited for SPDT applications, it is crucial to design them with functional group modifications and the proper metal ions.

In recent years, the use of nanomaterial-based drugs in cancer treatment has attracted considerable attention with the developments in materials science and nanotechnology. Thus, it has been possible to target anticancer drugs to cancer cells and increase their efficiency by loading them into a packaging system made of nanomaterials [28]. Furthermore, using hydrophilic nanoparticles to encapsulate the hydrophobic drugs may increase their solubility, thereby improving their in vivo bioavailability and thus allowing for more effective delivery [29].

Among the different classes of nanomaterials the carbon based quantum dots have been widely used in biomedical field [30]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a growing interest due to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water dispersibility and chemical stability providing them to be used in biological applications such as biosensor, bioimaging, medical diagnostic, drug carriers, photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy [31]. They can form non-covalent conjugates based on π-π interactions with π-electron rich molecules such as phthalocyanines due to their high surface area and planar π-electron rich structure. This conjugation has led to enhanced photophysical and photochemical properties [32–34].

In this study, firstly the designed phthalocyanine was synthesized and characterized. Then the GQDs was functionalized with cystein (cys) which is essential amino acid as the important bio-source. Finally the prepared new InPc was conjugated onto the cystein-functionalized GQDs (cys-GQDs). The photosensitive capabilities of the InPc alone and InPc-conjugated to the cys-GQDs (InPc@cys-GQDs) were determined based on their photochemical and sono-photochemical properties. This is the first example as a supramolecular ensembles incorporating both phthalocyanine and cys-GQDs and will shed light on similar studies.
All materials and equipment used in this study are detailed in the supplementary material.

Firstly 1 g 4-Nitrophthalonitrile (7.01 mmol) was dissolved with DMF under argon gas and 1 g 2- Chloro-4-methylphenol (7.01 mmol) was added. Following a 30-minute stir at room temperature, 2.90 g (21.04 mmol) of anhydrous potassium carbonate was added. Under argon gas at room temperature, the reaction was stirred for 24 h. The resulting mixture was then precipitated by adding it to 200 ml of ice water and subsequently filtered. The filtrate was rinsed extensively with water to eliminate potassium carbonates and then washed with methanol. The dried product was crystallized with an ethanol-methanol mixture under vacuum, resulting in a pure white product. Yield: 32 %. IR spectrum (cm−1): 3000–3100 (CH), 2232 (CN), 1590 (CC), 1250 (COC), 799 (C-Cl). 1H NMR (500 MHz, δ ppm, CDCI3): 7.75 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.73 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.57 (d, 2H, Ar-H), 7.08 (d, 2H, Ar-H), 1.58 (s, 3H, –CH3). MS (MALDI-MS) m/z: Calculated: 268.697 for C15H9ClN2O; Found: 270.883 [M + 2H] +.

Compound 1a (0.1 g, 0.37 mmol), indium chloride (0.05 g, 0.284 mmol), and 4 drops of DBU were combined with 4 mL of dry pentanol in a reaction tube. The mixture was heated under an argon atmosphere at 120 °C for 24 h. The green mixture was cooled to room temperature and then slowly added dropwise to 200 mL of n-hexane, causing precipitation. The precipitated core product was purified by washing with hot hexane, ethanol, and methanol. Finally, TLC was measured in the raw product. Chloroform was used as the running solvent. Yield: 52 %. IR spectrum (cm−1): 2922 (CH), 1589 (CC), 1206 (COC), 741 (C-Cl). 1H NMR (500 MHz, δ ppm, CDCI3): 7.35 (br.s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.21 (s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.15 (br.d, 8H, Ar-H), 7.08 (br.d, 8H, Ar-H), 1.28 (s, 12H, –CH3). MS (MALDI-MS)(dithranol) m/z: Calculated: 1225.06 for C60H36Cl4N8O4InCl; Found: 1189.51 [M−Cl]+.

Cys-GQDs were synthesized using with citric acid as a carbon source and Cystein amino acid as a sulfur and nitrogen source using the literature method with some modification [35]. Briefly, 1 g citric acid and 0.5 g cysteine were dissolved in 3 mL of DI water. The obtained solution was hydrothermally treated at 220 °C for 12 h under N2 gas. Afer that, the reddish-brown mixture was filtered. This suspension was purifed with a dialysis membrane (12–14 kD, Fisher Scientifc) against DI water for 24 h and then precipitated in excess amount of acetone by using a centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, cys-GQDs were dried in an oven at 50 °C and stored in a closed container for further applications.

The InPc conjugation of cys-GQDs based on π-π stacking was performed by way of a slight modification to the previously reported literature [36]. Briefly, the solution of 12.5 mg of cys-GQDs (in 1 mL H2O) and the solutions of 25 mg of InPc (0,5 mL in DMF) were sonicated for 1 h seperately. Then, cys-GQDs solution was added to the solutions of InPc and the mixture was sonicated for 4 h then stirred at room temperature for 4 d. The mixture was precipitated with ethanol and washed with water and ethanol. The product was dried in a laboratory oven at 60◦C to obtain InPc@cys-GQDs. The obtained nanocompozite is represented as InPc@cys-GQDs.
The fluorescence quantum yield, singlet oxygen quantum yield, and photodegradation quantum yield were assessed following the procedures detailed in the supplementary material.

In this study, our goal was to produce a new indium phthalocyanine which have high singlet oxygen efficiency. Scheme 1 portrays the synthetic route of creating indium phthalocyanine. The compound was prepared by combining 2-Chloro-4-methylphenol phthalonitrile (1a) and indium chloride in dry pentanol and DBU under controlled conditions outlined. Despite the typical insolubility of phthalocyanines in organic solvents, this compound demonstrated notable solubility in DMSO. The complex was characterized using UV–Vis, FT-IR, 1H NMR and MALDI-TOF techniques. Following characterization, the compound's photophysical and photochemical properties were examined to evaluate its potential for anti-cancer therapy.

In the second part of the study, the newly synthesized InPc was conjugated to cys-GQDs via π-π stacking. The performed conjugation procedure was achieved as represented in Scheme 2 . The structural features of the nanoassembly were evaluated by using analytical techniques such as XRD, FT-IR, dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV/vis by the comparing with the components (InPc alone and cys-GQDs alone). The results were confirmed the expected structures.

The UV–Vis spectra of phthalocyanine compounds display two noticeable bands known as Q and B. The Q band, found in the visible range between 600 and 700 nm, results from transitions of π → π∗. Meanwhile, the B band emerges at shorter wavelengths, between 300 and 500 nm [37,38]. In this study, UV–Vis absorption spectra of InPc observed Q-band absorptions at 694 nm and B-band absorptions at 336 nm in DMSO.

The ground state absorption spectrums were recorded in DMSO solution for cys-GQDs alone, InPc alone and the formed conjugate between them as InPc@cys-GQDs (Fig. 1 ). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of cys-GQDs shows a strong absorption peak at 344 nm, due to π-π* transition of aromatic structure and 392 nm belonging to n-π* transition of carbonyl groups and CS bonds arising from cys [39]. Phthalocyanines are characterized in the UV–visible spectra by two absorption bands that are associated with the heteroaromatic π-system of the ligand [40].

The Q band in the visible region is related to the π-π* transition resulting from the chromophoric system and was observed at 693 nm, while the other main absorption band, which is the so-called B band, was observed at 353 nm. Upon the conjugation between cys-GQDs and InPc, the obtained nanosystem exhibited the typical absorption bands confirming the presence of InPc and the cys-GQDs. The Q band of the InPc@cys-GQDs was 2 nm red-shifted when compared to the G2 alone, indicating the interaction between the two macrocycle [41]. Negligible changes in the Q-bands of Pcs upon non-covalent interaction with carbon nanomaterials are as expected and have been reported [42]. However, the intense absorption in the B band region of InPc@cys-GQDs is due to intense absorbance of cys-GQDs as shifting resulting from the ground-state electron donor–acceptor interaction between the InPc and cys-GQDs (Fig. 1).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to explore the morphology of the prepared products. While the SEM image of the InPc exhibited the agglomerated and stacked crystallites, the conjugated form of the InPc resulting as InPc@cys-GQDs displayed the microstructure as an undefined geometric shape [45].

DLS was employed to determination of particle size of cys-GQDs and its nanoconjugate forming as InPc@cys-GQDs. The average DLS sizes of cys-GQDs was determined to be 7.8 nm which is accordance with reported GQDs (Fig. 4 a) [46]. DLS result of the InPc@cys-GQDs showed the particle size to be 28.19 nm which is the higher in size as compared to cys-GQDs. This is the indication of the formation of supramolecular assemblies of the GQDs and InPc.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to characterize the newly synthesized InPc, cys-GQDs and the cys-GQDs–InPc conjugate (Fig. 5 ).

In the FT-IR spectrum of compound 1a, the characteristic vibration corresponding to CN was observed at 2232 cm−1. The aromatic CH peak was seen at between 3000–3100 cm−1. The other vibrations were at 1250 cm−1 for COC, 1590 cm−1 for CC and 799 cm−1 for C-Cl vibration. The compound 1a was cyclotetramerized using indium chloride to produce indium phthalocyanine derivatives. After conversion, the characteristic CN peaks disappeared on the FT-IR spectra of InPc due to the formation of phthalocyanine. The characteristic COC vibration was observed at 1206 cm−1, aromatic CH peak was seen at 2922 cm−1, CC were at 1589 cm−1 and C-Cl were at 741 cm−1 for InPc. 1HNMR spectra of the synthesized compounds, 1a: Protons found as 7.75 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.73 (s, 1H, Ar-H), 7.57 (d, 2H, ArH), 7.08–7.06 (d, 2H, Ar-H), 1.58 (s, 3H, –CH3) and their integral values are in harmony. InPc: Protons found 7.35 (br.s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.21 (s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.15 (br.d, 8H, Ar-H), 7.07 (br.d, 8H, Ar-H), 1.28 (s, 12H, –CH3) and their integral values are in harmony. There are broad peaks in the NMR data. This difficulty in NMR recording is due to the existence of isomeric mixtures and aggregation effects. The hypothesized structures were supported by the mass spectra of all synthesized phthalocyanine complexes, with the parent ions at m/z: 270.883 [M + 2H]+ for 1a, 1189.51 [M−CI]+ for InPc. The characterization spectra of all the synthesized compounds are illustrated in the supplementary information Fig.(S1-S6).

In the FTIR spectrum of cys-GQDs exhibited the functional groups localized at the edges/surface of graphene sheet. The band at 1709 cm−1 for cys-GQDs is attributed to CO vibration of the carboxyl group. The broad band arising in the range 3402 − 3196 cm−1 is due to the presence of functional groups like –OH and NH2. The peaks at 1635, 1530, 1393, 1232 and 1204 cm−1 can be attributed to NH bending, CC, CN, CO and CS stretching vibrations. In addition, two bands at 2986 cm−1 and 2939 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of –CH2 groups. After performing the conjugation between InPc and cys-GQDs to obtain InPc@cys-GQDs, the indicative peaks such as the CO vibration of the carboxyl group and the OH vibrations as the graphene functional groups revelaed at 1705 cm−1 and 3231 cm−1, respectively. The peaks associated with the CC, CN, CO and CS stretching vibrations were seen at 1574, 1385,1242 and 1207 cm−1, respectively. When compare the FT-IR spectra of the cys-GQDs alone, the shift of the peaks seen in the FT-IR of InPc@cys-GQDs prove the interaction. The peaks belonging to the InPc were observed also at the FT-IR of the conjugate confirming the loading of the InPcs (G2) onto the cys-GQDs.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was utilized for the structural characterization of cys-GQDs, InPc alone and InPc@cys-GQDs are shown in Fig. 6 . The observed main broad band with a maximum at 2θ=21.99° is due to (002) Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers, reflecting the small size of dots (Fig. 6a) [47]. The typical amorphous nature of the phthalocyanines was observed in the diffraction patterns of InPc at 2θ = 22.15° (Fig. 6b) [48]. InPc@cys-GQDs showed the similar diffraction peaks as broad at 2θ =21.08° revealing between two component of the conjugate signifying the interaction between cys-GQDs and InPc (G2) (Fig. 6c) [49].

The singlet oxygen quantum yield stands out as a paramount parameter evaluated to gauge a molecule's potential as a sensitizer in PDT/SPDT applications. A highly efficient sono/photosensitizer should generate effective singlet oxygen, as it plays a crucial role in inducing the death of tumor cells. Efficient intersystem crossing in a photosensitizer result in a significant population of triplet states and a prolonged lifetime, consequently boosting the generation of singlet oxygen. It is recognized that incorporating a heavy central metal atom increases the population of triplet state species [25,26]. Consequently, the design of the new indium phthalocyanine aims to achieve a high singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ).

In this study, singlet oxygen quantum yields were measured using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a singlet oxygen quencher. The measurements were conducted in solutions containing synthesized phthalocyanine compound and standard ZnPc, both dissolved in DMSO, an organic solvent. In photochemical studies, UV–Vis spectra were generated by exposing each sample to light with an intensity of 7.05 × 1015 photon s−1cm−2, with measurements taken at 5-second intervals. Absorption changes of the DPBF compound around the wavelength of approximately 417 nm were recorded. Since the DPBF compound is photosensitive and degrades when exposed to light, the preparation of all solutions and taking measurements were carried out in a dark room. The singlet oxygen quantum efficiencies of the synthesized compound are detailed in Table 1. In the photochemical studies of compound InPc, the singlet oxygen quantum yield was determined to be 0.65. Although the ΦΔ values are lower than those of unsubstituted ZnPc in DMSO (ΦΔ = 0.67) [52], they are still adequate for photodynamic therapy. In sono-photochemical studies, sample was subjected to ultrasound waves for 5 s followed by exposure to light at an intensity of 7.05 × 1015 photon s−1cm−2 for 5 s. This cycle was repeated 5 times, while monitoring the absorption changes of the DPBF compound around 417 nm and obtaining UV–Vis spectra. In sono-photochemical studies with compound InPc, the singlet oxygen quantum yield was calculated as 0.94. When comparing the results, it is evident that the singlet oxygen quantum yield obtained in sono-photochemical determination is significantly higher than that achieved through only photochemical methods. Despite the use of low ultrasound frequency in sono-photochemical determination, it markedly enhanced the singlet oxygen yield compared to only photochemical applications, demonstrating its suitability and preference as a method in cancer treatment. The UV–Vis spectra illustrating the singlet oxygen quantum yield measurements of the synthesized compounds given in Fig. 8 .

It was noted that the singlet oxygen yield increased in the measurements following the conjugation of synthesized phthalocyanine with quantum dots as expected. Because the conjugation of quantum dot with phthalocyanine reduces the fluorescence of aromatic molecules and thus increases the yield of singlet oxygen by increasing the rate of transition to the intersystem triplet state. Fluorescence quenching of aromatic molecules by graphene sheets has been reported due to the fact that the S1 excited state of Pc moiety in Pc–graphene complex is quenched somewhat by the bound graphene, which is the intramolecular interaction. This case could cause more efficient intersystem crossing to the triplet state, resulting in the more populated triplet excited photo sensitizer in this state and accordingly, more singlet oxygen quantum yield [53].

Although GQDs are known to have ability to produce singlet oxygen by itself [54], after conjugating with photosensitizers (PS), the GQDs photoluminescence properties are quenched upon interaction with PS molecules serves as the indicator of the nanocomposite (PS@GQD) formation. In addition to the quenching of the fluorescence leading to more populated triplet state, the other interpretation from the photochemical point of view that the generation of singlet oxygen by PS@GQD nanocomposites most probably involve two steps: (i) the energy transfer from GQD to PS followed by the nonradiative transition into the PS triplet state T1 and (ii) energy transfer PS (T1) oxygen (3O2) resulting in 1O2 generation [55].

Compound InPc@cys-GQDs yielded a singlet oxygen quantum yield of 0.79 in photochemical studies and 1.17 in sono-photochemical studies. The singlet oxygen quantum yields of compound InPc@cys-GQDs are given in Table 1. UV–Vis spectra of singlet oxygen quantum yield measurements of compound InPc@cys-GQDs are given in Fig. 9 . The ability of cys-GQDs to generate singlet oxygen independently has been demonstrated through photochemical and sono-photochemical studies, Fig. S7,S8. As evidenced by the spectral data, the conjugation of phthalocyanine has significantly enhanced the therapeutic efficacy of the molecule.

Additionally, Table 2 lists previously published data on several graphene quantum dots. The quantum yields obtained in this research are compared with those of other synthesized compounds reported in the literature. The achieved ΦΔPDT result of the compound InPc@cys-GQDs is higher than the existing data, which differ in the range of 0.30 to 0.69 for all complexes from [56,58], while the ΦΔPDT result of the compound InPc@cys-GQDs is lower than those of all complexes from [57]. Considering these results, the newly synthesized phthalocyanines in this study exhibit significantly high quantum yields, therefore, they demonstrate potential for use as sensitizers in cancer treatment through photodynamic and sono-photodynamic therapy.

Photodegradation quantum yield (Φd) is a measure that quantitatively expresses the efficiency with which a substance undergoes degradation or decomposition upon exposure to light. This parameter is important for assessing the photochemical stability or instability of phthalocyanines under various light conditions and indicates the potential for the molecule to be used as a photosensitizer in PDT [52,59]. In this part of the study, to show that the synthesized phthalocyanine can be used in photochemical studies, its stability against light was examined. To measure the photodegradation quantum yield, the synthesized compounds were dissolved in DMSO, and the solution was exposed to light at an intensity of 2.42x1016 s−1cm−2 in 5-minute intervals repeated 5 times. Absorption changes within the 600–700 nm range, corresponding to the Q band, were monitored, and UV–Vis spectra were acquired. The photodegradation quantum yield was calculated as Φd = 20 x10−5 for InPc, and Φd = 12 x10−5 for InPc@cys-GQDs, Table 1. It was observed that the cysteine functionalized GQDs (cys-GQDs) was more stable towards light irradiation. According to the literature, the photodegradation capacities of stable complexes typically range between 10−6 and 10−3 [60,61]. This outcome demonstrates that the synthesized complexes exhibit high stability under light exposure, ensuring their suitability for applications in PDT and SPDT. The compounds remain intact and do not undergo decomposition when subjected to light, highlighting their reliability for these therapeutic uses. The changes in the UV–Vis spectra resulting from the photodegradation studies of the synthesized complex are depicted in Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 .

The aim of this study is to synthesize a new indium phthalocyanine and its cys-GQDs derivative with high singlet oxygen yield that can be used in photodynamic and sono-photodynamic therapy. Following the characterization of InPc, it was immobilized on quantum dots to enhance singlet oxygen production, and then the photochemical and sono-photochemical properties of both InPc and InPc@cys-GQDs were analyzed. When the methods used in this study were compared, it was observed that the singlet oxygen yields from the sono-photochemical study were higher than that obtained from the photochemical study for both substances. Upon examining the contribution of the molecule used, it was observed that the conjugated phthalocyanine exhibited a higher singlet oxygen quantum yield in both methods. This is because the presence of graphene quantum dots reduces the fluorescence quantum yield and therefore leads to intersystem crossing resulting in a greater number of triplet species, which in turn leads to the formation of more singlet oxygen. Furthermore, the photodegradation studies on the synthesized phthalocyanines showed that they were stable under light conditions.
Considering these results, it can be stated that the synthesized compounds may be good candidates as sono/phototherapeutic sensitizers.

Gökçe Gökçil: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation. Kevser Celep: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Pınar Şen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Fikrettin Şahin: Writing – review & editing. Ali Erdoğmuş: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Göknur Yaşa Atmaca: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Yildiz Technical University, Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit (Project No: FCD-2023-5812).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2025.117468.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sinoporphyrin sodium triggered sono-photodynamic effects on breast cancer both in vitro and in vivo
